content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural grade reorganization (lower grades stay, upper grades centralize)'. Portugal's experience shows that school closure intensity from 2005–2014 did not automatically improve learning outcomes, and conditions of consolidation (travel burden, facility quality, community voice, pedagogical support) matter more for rural student access and equity than closure decisions alone.
opportunities: Enables full-time specialist subject teaching at the centralized upper-grade site, addressing the documented Polish problem of rural teachers commuting between multiple part-time postings.

Preserves a local school presence for the youngest, most travel-vulnerable children (grades 1-4/1-6), partially mitigating the documented burden of early-morning long-distance travel for young pupils.
strengths: Portugal closed 4,197 primary schools between 2005/2006 and 2012/2013, initially targeting schools with fewer than 10 students (2005), then expanding to schools with fewer than 21 students (2010).

In Portugal's Central Region, 90% of all school closures (1,981 establishments) occurred in 2005–2014, with the process stabilizing after 2015; the Viseu Dão Lafões inter-municipal area alone lost 527 establishments (2005–2022).
threats: Poland's 1999 reform was politically reversible and was in fact reversed after 17 years; without concrete cross-party entrenchment mechanisms or constitutional-level protection, Hungary's version faces similar reversal risk, especially given the documented absence of any cross-party consensus mechanism for rural school reform.

Teacher redeployment under Act LII of 2023 (Status Law), which grants district centers authority to reassign teachers, may trigger the kind of mobilization seen in the Tanítanék movement if perceived as involuntary displacement without adequate consultation.
weaknesses: To what extent did Portugal's school closures directly cause village depopulation, versus depopulation driving closure demand? The causal direction is contested in existing sources (sociologist advocacy claims versus no quantitative longitudinal study).

What proportion of Hungarian depopulating villages have the demographic and geographic characteristics (sub-2,000 population, travel distance > 25 minutes to next school) that would align with Portuguese closure scenarios? No baseline census data on rural school catchment distance has been cited.
